title: I Don't Understand
description: The phrases every foreigner needs before anything else

scenarios.situation: The Foreign Police officer says something in Slovak you completely missed. What is the first thing you say?
scenarios.answerMeaning: Nerozumiem = I don't understand — your first response when you miss something. Short, clear, and universally understood.

scenarios.situation: The officer continues speaking Slovak very fast. What do you ask?
scenarios.answerMeaning: Hovorte pomaly, prosím = Please speak slowly. The most useful phrase when someone is speaking too fast — and it works in any situation.

scenarios.situation: The doctor uses a medical term you have never heard. What do you ask?
scenarios.answerMeaning: Čo to znamená? = What does that mean? — essential when you hear a word you don't know. Doctors, officers and officials will usually explain simply.

scenarios.situation: The landlord is explaining your contract over the phone very quickly. You need them to write it down. What do you ask?
scenarios.answerMeaning: Môžete to napísať? = Can you write it down? — when phone Slovak is too fast or unclear, asking them to write it gives you time to translate and process.

scenarios.situation: You are at the supermarket checkout and the cashier asks you something. You don't understand. What do you say first?
scenarios.answerMeaning: Nerozumiem — always your first response when you miss something. The cashier will likely repeat more slowly or show you the screen.

scenarios.situation: You call 155 and the dispatcher speaks too fast. What do you ask?
scenarios.answerMeaning: Even in an emergency — asking the dispatcher to speak slowly is the right move. A calm, clear exchange saves time. Dispatchers are trained for this.

scenarios.situation: A colleague sends you a Slovak message and you don't recognise one word. You reply asking what it means. What do you write?
scenarios.answerMeaning: Čo to znamená? = What does that mean? — works in writing too. Simple and direct.

scenarios.situation: Someone is speaking to you and you need a few seconds to check your translation app. What do you say?
scenarios.answerMeaning: Moment, prosím = Just a moment, please — buys you time without being rude. Use it whenever you need a few seconds to think, check your phone, or collect yourself.

items.translation: Excuse me, ___. Do you speak English?
___ Slovak. Can you write it down?
Please speak ___. I don't understand fast speech.
What does that ___? I don't know this word.
___, please. I need to translate this.
Can you ___? I didn't understand the first time.

scenarios.situation: You are at the Foreign Police. The officer says something. You say 'Nerozumiem.' They repeat faster. What is your next move?
scenarios.answerMeaning: You have tried Nerozumiem. The next step is to ask if they speak English. Many Foreign Police officers do. If not, then ask them to write it down.

scenarios.situation: The officer does not speak English. You have now tried everything verbal. What is your final option?
scenarios.answerMeaning: Môžete to napísať? = Can you write it down? — your last resort when verbal communication fails. Written Slovak is easier to translate with your phone.

scenarios.situation: You are leaving the Foreign Police office. You want to say goodbye formally. (the Greetings lesson)
scenarios.answerMeaning: Dovidenia = Goodbye (formal) — always use formal farewell in official settings. From the Greetings lesson.
